Facts.text: Pope Clement V closes the Council of Vienne
Renaissance masterpiece The Ghent Altarpiece by Hubert and Jan van Eyck is consecrated at St Bravo's Cathedral, Belgium, commissioned by wealthy merchants
Albert VII Archduke of Austria and Isabella, daughter of Philip II of Spain granted rule of the Habsburg Netherlands (after they marry)
Leon VII Spanish poet's first poem is published: La Cocina
Dutch colonist Peter Minuit organizes the purchase of Manhattan Island from Native Americans for 60 guilders worth of goods, believed to be the Canarsee Indians of the Lenape
Johan Mauritius resigns as governor of Brazil
Brandenburg monarch Frederik Willem signs treaty with Netherlands
First international boxing match: Local fighter Bob Whittaker beats “The Venetian Gondolier”, Tito di Carni at James Figg's academy amphitheatre in Marylebone, London
French King Louis XV observes transit of Mercury at Mendon Castle
Battle of Prague: Frederick II of Prussia's forces defeat Austrian army
1st African American Masonic Lodge (African # 459) forms Prince Hall, Boston
Suriname sold to Great Britain (until Feb 1816)
Dutch politician Johan Thorbecke argues for the general right to vote
Otto Tank ends slavery in Suriname colony
American physician and inventor John Gorrie patents a "refrigeration machine" to make ice
New slave regulations go into effect in Suriname
San Francisco Chamber of Commerce starts
1st major US rail disaster kills 46 at Norwalk, Connecticut
San Francisco Olympic Club, 1st US athletic club forms
Arkansas and Tennessee become the 9th and 10th states to secede from the US
Battle of Port Walthall Junction, fought in Virginia begins (Battle of Port Walthall Junction), Union victory (US Civil War)
Thomas Henry Burke and Lord Frederick Cavendish are stabbed and killed during the Phoenix Park Murders in Dublin
US Congress passes the Chinese Exclusion Act, halting Chinese immigration
Conductors on London General Omnibus Company go on strike

Samuel Pierpont Langley flies his unpiloted Number 5 aircraft using a catapult launch from a boat on the Potomac River, USA. The aircraft travels almost 3/4 of a mile - ten times further than any previous heavier-than-air flying machine.
British SS Camorta sinks in a cyclone in the Gulf of Martaban near Rangoon with the loss of about 739 lives
Zulu assault at Holkrantz, South Africa
Chicago White Sox commit 11 errors against Detroit Tigers but win 10-9 at South Side Park. Chicago
American Lung Association holds its 1st meeting
"Temporary" permit to erect overhead wires on Market Street, San Francisco allows United Railroads to run electric streetcars
Indian nationalist Sri Aurobindo acquitted in the Alipore Bomb Case in Calcutta, India
King Nikita I of Montenegro vacates Skoetari, northern Albania
British House of Lords rejects women's suffrage
German U-20 sinks Centurion south east of Ireland
The Allies on Cape Helles launch three attacks to enlarge their beachheads; after terrible losses, they advance about three miles
Belgian troops march into Kigali, German East Africa
St Louis Browns pitcher Bob Groom no-hits Chicago White Sox, 3-0 at Sportsman's Park, St. Louis
Paris Peace Conference disposes of German colonies; German East Africa is assigned to Britain and France, German South West Africa to South Africa
Teams from the National Association Football League and the Southern New England Soccer League merge to form the American Soccer League (ASL); collapses 1933
AL announces it will discontinue MVP award
New York to San Francisco footrace begins
Italy and USSR sign trade agreement
Audrey Wurdemann is the youngest person at 24 to be awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry for "Bright Ambush"
KTM-AM in Los Angeles California changes call letters to KEHE (now KABC)
Dutch court sentences writer Maurits Dekker to 50 days imprisonment for "offending a friendly head of state" - Adolf Hitler
Corregidor & Philippines surrender to Japanese Armies
British 1st army opens assault on Tunis
KJR-AM in Seattle Washington swaps calls with KOMO
General Johannes Blaskowitz surrenders German troops in Netherlands
World War II: Axis Sally delivers her last propaganda broadcast to Allied troops (first was on December 11, 1941).
Pitts Pirate Cliff Chambers no-hits Boston Brave, 3-0
MLB St. Louis Browns Alva "Bobo" Holloman, in his first start game as starting pitcher, no-hits visiting Philadelphia A's, 6-0
West Germany joins NATO
Gus Bell (Reds) homers off Bob Miller in both ends of a double header
WRCB TV channel 3 in Chattanooga, TN (NBC) begins broadcasting
Last broadcast of "I Love Lucy" on CBS-TV
Iceland gunboats shoot at British fishing vessels
Students attack Dutch embassy in Jakarta
Trotsky's murderer Jacques Mornard (Ramon Mercader) freed from prison after serving 19 years 8 months in Mexico
Omer Vanaudenhove chosen chairman of Belgium Liberal Party
1st nuclear warhead fired from Polaris submarine (Ethan Allen)
Antonio Segnielected President of Italy
Pathet Lao breaks cease fire and conquer Nam Tha Laos
US performs nuclear test at Pacific Ocean
Pulitzer prize awarded to Barbara Tuchman (Guns of August)
Joe Orton's play "Entertaining Mr Sloan" premieres in London
Bill Lawry (210) andBob Simpson(201) complete record opening stand of 382 in Australia's 1st innings, drawn 4th Test v West Indies in Bridgetown
Canadian Minister of Finance announces a $20 Centennial gold coin
Most runs scored in 11th inning (9) Phils score 5 to beat Pirates 8-7
Myra Hindley and Ian Brady are sentenced to life imprisonment for the Moors Murders in England
400 students seize administration building at Cheyney State College, Pennsylvania
Maureen Wilton runs female world record marathon (3:15:22)
Zakir Husain elected 1st Muslim President of India
Giants reliever Lindy McDaniel sets NL record of 225th consecutive errorless game (108 chances consecutively since June 16, 1964)
Spain closes border to Gibraltar except to Spaniards
Street battle between students and troops in Paris, 1,000 injured
Yuchiro Miura of Japan skis down Mt Everest
Deniz Gezmiş, Yusuf Aslan and Hüseyin İnan are executed in Ankara for attempting to overthrow the Constitutional order
1st WHA championship, New England Whalers beat Win Jets, 4 games to 1
Bundy victim Roberta Parks disappears from OSU, Corvallis, Ore
Oakland A's pitcher Paul Lindblad makes an errant throw in 1st inning of 6-3 loss to Baltimore, ending his record streak of 385 consecutive errorless games
Scotland Yard recovers stolen Johannes Vermeer oil painting "The Guitar Player" at  the cemetery of St Bartholomew-the-Great, in London's financial
Smallest attendance at Philadelphia's Veterans Stadium (4,149)
3 people die in tornado that strikes Omaha, Nebraska
Bundy victim Lynette Culver disappears from Pocatello, Idaho
Early warnings provided by REACT (ham radio operators) means only
An earthquake strikes Friuli in Northern Italy, causes 989 deaths and the destruction of entire villages
Parlophone releases live album "The Beatles at the Hollywood Bowl" in UK; recorded during 1964 and 1965 tours, sound quality was of sub-par quality, but released to combat bootleg versions
South Africa military goes into Angola
American Fred Markham (22) sets a bicycle speed record of 81.8 kph (50.8 mph) over 200 meter course
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
US expels Libyan diplomats
Seattle MarinersGaylord Perrybecomes 15th pitcher to win 300 games
Baltimore OrioleCal Ripken Jrhits for cycle
Jose Napoleon Duarte wins El Salvador presidential election
17th NASA Space Shuttle Mission (51-B): Challenger 7 lands at Edwards AFB
Donald E. Pelotte becomes the first Native American Catholic bishop
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
American politician Gary Hart denies affair with model Donna Rice
American televangelistJim Bakkerand Rich Dortch dismissed from Assemblies of God after revelations of an alleged rape of a church secretary
Niroslav Milhailovic begins 54 hours of telling jokes
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Doughnutgate incident: NJ Devils' coach Jim Schoenfeld tells referee Don Koharski to 'eat another doughnut you fat pig!,' he is suspended
Graeme Hick scores 405 for Worcs v Somerset 35 fours 11 sixes
Tom Cruiseis ticketed for careless operation of a vehicle in South Carolina
Phillie Lenny Dykstra slams his sports car into 2 trees
Seppo Raty of Finland sets javelin record to 301' 9"
Space Shuttle STS 39 (Discovery 12) lands
NY MetAnthony Youngbegins losing streak of at least 26 games
STS-55 (Columbia) lands
Comedian Bobcat Goldthwait sets fire to the guest chair on NBC's "The Tonight Show"
US House of Representatives passes the Federal Assault Weapons Ban
Classic Sports Network begins on cable TV
Body of former CIA Director William Colby is found washed up on a riverbank in southern Maryland eight days after his disappearance
Guatemala's leftist guerrillas sign key accord with government of President Alvaro Arzu aimed at ending 35 years of civil war
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Hartford, Connecticut on WCCC 106.9 FM
NHL Hartford Whalers become Carolina Hurricanes
Rick Pitino becomes coach of Boston Celtics
The Bank of England is given independence from political control, the most significant change in the bank's 300-year history
"The Good Stuff" single released by Kenny Chesney (Billboard Song of the Year 2002)
Dutch politician Pim Fortuyn is shot and killed at close range with an illegally purchased pistol by an animal rights activist upset with Fortuyn's policies
The Rolling Stonesfly a yellow blimp emblazoned with their logo at Van Cortlandt Park in the Bronx, NYC to announce forthcoming international tour
Chaiten Volcano erupts in Chile, forcing the evacuation of more than 4,500 people
Marco van Bastenresigns as manager of Ajax after the team fails to qualify for the Champions League
The US Department of Labor states that 244,000 jobs were created in April, with 235,000 added in February and 221,000 in March, but unemployment continues to grow, reaching 9%
Greek parliamentary election results in 60% support for parties opposed to austerity measures
10 people are killed and 26 people are injured after a wave of attacks across Iraq
13 people are killed in clashes with Bangladeshi police in Dhaka
15 people are killed and 50 are injured after a bomb detonates at a political rally in Pakistan
Brazilian neobank Nubank founded in Brazil by David Vélez, Cristina Junqueira and Edward Wible
US Senate passes a bill enabling the taxation of online sales
Wal-Mart becomes the largest company by revenue on the Fortune 500 list
Boko Haram's leader, Abubakar Shekau, releases a 57 minute video boasting about abducting schoolgirls in Nigeria
84 abducted schoolgirls released in exchange for Boko Haram suspects in Nigeria
Bus crash in Arusha region, Tanzania kills 35
France bans too thin fashion models and makes labeling of digitally enhanced photos mandatory

French telecom company Orange and its former CEO with other executives go on trial in France for psychological harassment of their staff that unions claim resulted in 19 suicides and 12 other attempts
One million plant and animal species are now at risk of extinction according to a major new UN report
Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah of Brunei announces a moratorium on the death penalty for gay sex and adultery after worldwide condemnation over new Sharia laws
World Snooker Championship, Crucible Theatre, Sheffield: Englishman Judd Trump wins his first world crown beating John Higgins of Scotland, 18-9
At least 90,000 healthcare workers worldwide infected by COVID-19, more than 260 nurses have died according to International Council of Nurses
Former intelligence chief Mustafa al-Kadhimi sworn in as new Iraqi Prime Minister, 3rd to be nominated in six months

Irish organization repays a 170 year old favor, raising over $2 million (to date) for US Navajo Nation and Hopi Reservation badly affected by COVID-19. In 1840s Choctaw Nation sent $170 to aid Irish potato famine.
UK becomes the first European country to report over 30,000 confirmed cases of COVID-19
Armed police raid on drug traffickers kills 25 in cities deadliest ever, in a favela of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
Explosion at the five-star Hotel Saratoga due to suspected a gas leak in Havana, Cuba kills at least 35 people
Canadian province Alberta declares a state of emergency over unprecedented wildfires, more than 100, that have forced the evacuation of 25,000 people
Coronation of King Chares III and Queen Camilla at Westminster Abbey, London - first monarch crowned in the UK in 70 years
Brandon Som wins the Pulitzer Prize for Poetry for "Tripas: Poems"
Pulitzer Prize for Fiction won by Jayne Anne Phillips for "Night Watch" set after the Civil War
Battle of Edington:Alfred the Greatand his West Saxon army defeat Viking army of Guthrum the Old [earliest possible date]
Spanish and German Imperial troops sack Rome, ending the Renaissance.Charles III, Duke of Bourbonis killed in the assault removing any restraint for the victorious soldiers.
Battle at Gogra: Mughal EmperorBaburbeats Afghans and Bengals
KingHenry VIIIorders a bible in English be placed in every church in England
Francis Xavierreaches Old Goa, then capital of Portuguese India
Louis XIVof France moves his court to Versailles
In HaitiToussaint Louverturechanges sides, ambushing his former ally the Spanish at San Raphael
James Gordon Bennett, Sr. publishes the first issue of theNew York Herald(price 1 cent)
US blacksmithJohn Deerecreates the first steel plough in Grand Detour, Illinois
Linus Yale Jr.patents the Yale cylinder lock
Giuseppe Garibaldi's Mille sets sail from Genoa to the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies
Battle of Wilderness, fought in Virginia, Confederate GeneralJames Longstreetseriously injured (Overland Campaign), inconclusive result (US Civil War)
US Civil War: Union ArmyGeneral Shermanbegins advance to Atlanta, Georgia, during the Atlanta Campaign
Epping Forest, England, dedicated byQueen Victoria
TsarNicolas IIof Russia claims right to legislate by decree and restricts the power of the Duma (Russian Parliament)
Future Baseball Hall of Fame sluggerBabe Ruthhits his first MLB home run; pitches 12 frames in Boston Red Sox 4-3 extra innings loss to New York Yankees
Ty Cobbhits his 5th HR in 2 games tying Cap Ansons record of 1884
MLB Boston Red Sox score 12 runs in 4th inning, including record 4 consecutive triples hit by Carl Reynolds, Moose Solters,Rick Ferrell, and B Walters
British KingGeorge Vand Queen Mary celebrate their Silver Jubilee
Pulitzer prize awarded toJohn Steinbeckfor "The Grapes of Wrath"
At California's March Field,Bob Hopeperforms his first USO show
Joseph Stalinbecomes Premier of the Soviet Union, replacing Foreign MinisterVyacheslav Molotov
Pulitzer Prize for History awarded toArthur M. Schlesinger Jr.for his novel "The Age of Jackson"
English athleteRoger Bannisterbecomes the first to run a sub-4-minute mile, recording 3:59.4 at Iffley Road Track in Oxford
Italian government ofAntonio Segniresigns
Pulitzer Prize for Biography awarded toJohn F. Kennedyfor "Profiles in Courage"
Rajendra Prasadre-elected President of India for a second term in a landslide
US PresidentEisenhowersigns Civil Rights Act of 1960
Jerry Sloanis selected by the Baltimore Bullets as the fourth overall pick of the 1965 NBA draft
The Rolling Stonesrelease the single "Paint It Black" in the US, featuring prominent sitar music played byBrian Jones
Columbia Records releases "Johnny Cashat Folsom Prison", the first live album by American singer-songwriter Johnny Cash, recorded in January 1968

Northern Ireland Prime MinisterJames Chichester-Clarkannounces an amnesty for all offences associated with demonstrations since 5 October 1968, resulting in the release of, among others,Ian Paisleyand Ronald Bunting
Irish Prime MinisterJack Lynchsacks two ministers in the Irish government over allegations of illegal arms importation
American composerRoger Sessionsreceives special Pulitzer Prize for his life's work in music
West German ChancellorWilly Brandtresigns amidst controversy over his aide Günter Guillaume's ties with the Stasi (East German secret service)

Columbia Records releases "Rhythm & Romance", the fifth studio album by American singer-songwriterRosanne Cash; produced by husband Rodney Crowell, the album was her first to feature her songwriting on a majority of the tracks
Mario Andrettisets one-lap speed record at Indy at 218.204 MPH
Former presidentP. W. Bothaquits South Africa's ruling National Party
British-Canadian boxerLennox Lewis(28) TKOs American Phil Jackson (29) in 8th round for WBC heavyweight title at Convention Hall in Atlantic City, New Jersey
Nelson Mandelaand the ANC, finally confirmed winners in South Africa's first post apartheid election

12th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees: TheBee Gees; Buffalo Springfield; Crosby, Stills & Nash; The Jackson Five;Joni Mitchell; Parliament-Funkadelic; The (Young) Rascals;Mahalia Jackson; Bill Monroe; and Syd Nathan
Kerry Wood strikes out 20 Houston Astros to tie the major league record held byRoger Clemens. He threw a one-hitter and did not walk a batter in his 5th career start.
During a trip to Syria, PopeJohn Paul IIbecomes the first pope to enter a mosque
EntrepreneurElon MuskfoundsSpaceX
TV sitcom "Friends" airs the series finale of its 10th and final season in the US, attracting 52.5 million viewers
"Shrek the Third" directed by Chris Miller and Raman Hui with voices byMike Myers,Cameron DiazandEddie Murphypremieres in Westwood Ca.
François Hollandeelected 24th President of France
Pablo Sandoval becomes second MLB player since 1900 to throw a scoreless outing, hit a home run and steal a base in the same game in SF Giants' 12-4 loss in Cincinnati; joinsChristy Mathewson1905
British prime ministerKeir Starmerand Indian prime ministerNarendra Modisign a 'landmark' free trade agreement to lower tariffs on 99% of Indian exports to the UK
World's first adhesive postage stamp, the "Penny Black", is first used in Great Britain

Exposition Universelle (World's Fair) in Paris opens with the recently completed Eiffel Tower serving as the entrance arch; the lifts in the tower are not ready, so intrepid visitors have to climb 1,710 steps to reach the top
George Vbecomes King of the United Kingdom upon the death of his father, Edward VII
German airship Hindenburg explodes in flames at Lakehurst, New Jersey, killing 35 of the 97 on board and one on the ground
Channel Tunnel linking Folkestone, England and Calais, France officially opens
Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor (1014-24) and King of Germany (1002-24), born in Abbach, Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1024)
Sejong the Great, 4th King of Joseon (1418-50), born in Hanseong, Kingdom of Joseon (d. 1450)
Marcellus II [Marcello Cervini], Italian humanist and Pope (1555, 22 days), born in Montefano, Marche, Papal States (d. 1555)
Frans Francken the Younger, Flemish painter, born in Antwerp (d. 1642)
Lorenzo Lippi [Perlone Zipoli], Italian poet and painter, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1664)
Henry Capell, 1st Baron Capell, English politician and First Lord of the British Admiralty, born in Hadham Parva, Hertfordshire (d. 1696)
Alain-René Lesage, French author (Turcaret ou le Financier), born in Sarzeau, France (d. 1747)
Charles Batteux, French philosopher, born in Alland'Huy-et-Sausseuil, Ardennes (d. 1780)
François Andrieux, French playwright, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1833)
Ferdinand III, Grand Duke of Tuscany (1790-1801), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1824)
Karl Christian Friedrich Krause, German philosopher (panentheism), and writer, born in Eisenberg, Rhenish Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1832)
Ludwig Borne [Loeb Baruch], German-Jewish writer, born in Frankfurt (d. 1837)
Wilhelm Würfel, Bohemian pianist, conductor, teacher, and composer, born in Plaňány, Bohemia (d. 1832)
Joseph Brackett, American religious leader (Shakers) and composer (Simple Gifts), born in Cumberland, Maine (d. 1882)
Ferdinand Marcucci, Italian harpist and composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1871)
Roman Sanguszko, Polish aristocrat and general, born in Volhynia (d. 1881)
George Sears Greene, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Apponaug, Rhode Island (d. 1899)
Wilhelm Schirmer, German landscape artist, born in Berlin (d. 1866)
Chapin A. Harris, American dentist who founded the America Society of Dental Surgeons, born in Pompey, New York (d. 1860)
William Walker, American composer (The Southern Harmony), born in Martin's Mills, Cross Keys, South Carolina (d. 1875)
Martin Delany, African-American abolitionist and physician (1st African American major in US Army), born in Charles Town, Virginia (d. 1885)
Joseph Tarr Copeland, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in New Castle, Maine (d. 1893)
Tokugawa Iesada, 13th Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1853-8), born in Edo, Japan (d. 1858)
Joseph Bailey, American civil engineer and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Morgan County, Ohio (d. 1867)
Phoebe Ann Coffin Hanaford, 1st female ordained minister in New England, born in Siasconset, Massachusetts (d. 1921)
Alfred Pease, American composer, born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1882)
Grove Karl Gilbert, American geologist (investigated Lake Bonneville, Utah), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1918)
Wyatt Eaton, Canadian-American artist, born in Philipsburg, Quebec (d. 1896)
Aristide Bruant [Bruand], French cabaret singer, chanson réaliste composer, comedian, and Monmartre nightclub owner, born in Courtenay, France (d. 1925)
Georges Hüe, French composer, born in Versailles, France (d. 1948)
Luis María Drago, Argentine statesman and author of Drago Doctrine, born in Buenos Aires (d. 1921)
Willem Kloos, Dutch poet (Act of Simple Justice), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1938)
Gaston Leroux, French writer (The Phantom of the Opera), born in Paris (d. 1927)
Joseph Cuvelier, Belgian historian and general archivist, born in Bilzen, Belgium (d. 1947)
John T. McCutcheon, American cartoonist (Pulitzer Prize-1931), born in South Raub, Tippecanoe County, Indiana (d. 1949)
August Reusner, German composer, born in Löwenberg, Silesia (d. 1935)
Christian Morgenstern, German poet (Ich und die Welt), born in Munich (d. 1914)
Victor Grignard, French chemist (Nobel 1912 - development of the Grignard reaction), born in Cherbourg, France (d. 1935)
Willem de Sitter, Dutch scientist and cosmologist (developed theoretical models of the universe based on Albert Einstein’s general theory of relativity), born in Sneek, Netherlands (d. 1934)
William D. Leahy, American admiral and chief of staff (1949), born in Hampton, Iowa (d. 1959)
Bedřich Hrozný, Czech orientalist and linguist (development of Hittitology), born in Lysá nad Labem, Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1952)
Tobias Norlind, Swedish musicologist, born in Vellinge, Sweden (d. 1947)
Edmund Ironside, 1st Baron Ironside, British fieldmarshal, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1959)
Georgi Atanasov, Bulgarian composer, noted as the nation's first opera composer (Gergana), born in Plovdiv, Bulgaria (d. 1931)
Wilhelm, the last Crown Prince of the German Empire and the Kingdom of Prussia, born in Marmorpalais, Potsdam, German Empire (d. 1951)
José Ortega y Gasset, Spanish philosopher and author (The Revolt of the Masses), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1955)
Victor Neuberg, English poet and publisher (discovered Dylan Thomas), born in London, England (d. 1940)
Emanuel Celler, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from New York), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1981)
Russell Stover, American chemist, entrepreneur, and co-founder of Russell Stover Candies with his wife Clara Stover, born in Alton, Kansas (d. 1954)
Stanley Morison, British typographer, born in Wanstead, Essex (d. 1967)
Claire Whitney, American silent film actress (Blind Fools, Haunted Mine), born in New York City (d. 1969)
Filip Lazar, Romanian composer, born in Craiova, Romania (d. 1936)
Fidél Pálffy, Hungarian National socialist (leading supporter of Nazism in Hungary), born in Szentgyörgy, Austria-Hungary (d. 1946)
Júlio César de Mello e Souza, Brazilian writer (The Man Who Counted), born in Rio de Janeiro (d. 1974)
Paul Alverdes, German writer (Pfeiferstube), born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1979)
Daniel Frank Gerber, American manufacturer of baby food, born in Fremont, Michigan (d. 1974)
Jascha Horenstein, Russian-born American conductor, born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1973)
Billy Cotton, British bandleader and entertainer, born in Smith Square, London (d. 1969)
Harry Golden, Jewish-American journalist and writer (Only in America), born in Mikulintsy, Ukraine (d. 1981)
Max Ophüls [Maximillian Oppenheimer], German-French film director (Letter from an Unknown Woman; La Ronde), born in Saarbrücken, German Empire (d. 1957)
Cliff Carlisle, American country and blues musician, singer, songwriter, and yodeler ("Goin' Down The Road Feelin' Bad"), born in Taylorsville, Kentucky (d. 1983)
Catherine Lacey, British actress (Sorcerors), born in London, England (d. 1979)
Harry Martinson, Swedish novelist and poet (Trade Wind-Nobel 1974), born in Jämshög, Sweden (d. 1978)
Moshé Feldenkrais, Ukrainian-Israeli engineer and physicist (founder of the Feldenkrais method), born in Slavuta, Russian Empire (d. 1984)
Raymond Bailey, American actor (Vertigo, The Beverly Hillbillies, The Incredible Shrinking Man), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1980)
Bernard "Toots" Shor, New York restaurateur (Toots Shor's Restaurant), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1977)
André Weil, French mathematician (foundational work in number theory and algebraic geometry), born in Paris (d. 1998)
Enrique Laguerre, Puerto Rican writer (La Llamarada), born in Moca, Puerto Rico (d. 2005)
Weeb Ewbank, American NFL coach (Baltimore Colts, NY Jets), born in Richmond, Indiana (d. 1998)
Necil Kazım Akses, Turkish classical composer, born in Istanbul, Turkey (d. 1999)
Loyd Sigmon, American radio broadcaster, sound engineer, and inventor ("Sig Alert" for traffic and emergency notices), born in Stigler, Oklahoma (d. 2004)
Antoon Breyne, Belgian journalist, born in Ypres, Belgium (d. 1986)
B. Roest Crollius, Dutch painter and writer (Chronicle Sins of Youth), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2000)
Hugh Martell, British Vice Admiral, born in Penzance, Cornwall, England (d. 1998)
William "Bill" Quinn, American actor (Star Trek, The Birds, Rifleman, All in the Family), born in New York City (d. 1994)
Carmen Cavallaro, American actor and pianist (Hollywood Canteen; Diamond Horseshoe), born in New York City (d. 1989)
Gyula David, Hungarian composer (Viola Concerto) and violinist, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1977)
Ronald Harris, British 1st Church Estates Commissioner, born in Cornwall Gardens, Kensington, London (d. 1995)
Stewart Granger, English actor (Prisoner of Zenda, King Solomon's Mines), born in London, England (d. 1993)
Freddie Cochrane, American boxer (World Welterweight champion 1941-1946), born in Elizabeth, New Jersey (d. 1993)
George Perle, American composer (12 Tone Tonality; Pulitzer Prize, 1986), born in Bayonne, New Jersey (d. 2009)
John Arnold, British judge and President of the Family Division of the High Court (1979-88), born in England (d. 2004)
May Henriquez-Alvarez, Curacao Dutch sculptor, born in Curacao (d. 1999)
Theodore H. White, American journalist (Making of President, Pulitzer Prize 1962), born in Dorchester, Boston (d. 1986)

Adriana Caselotti, American actress and singer, best known as the voice of Snow White in Walt Disney's animated film "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs" (1937), born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 1997)
Kal Mann, American lyricist ("Let's Twist Again"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2001)
Godfrey Ridout, Canadian composer, conductor, educator, and musicologist, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1984)
Sydney Chatton, English actress (Once Upon a Horse), born in Bolton, Lancashire, England (d. 1966)
Frank Ereaut, Bailiff of Jersey (1975-85), born in London (d. 1998)
Alan Dewitt, American actor (It's About Time), born in Canton, Ohio (d. 1976)
Elizabeth Sellars, Scottish actress ("Chalk Garden"; "A Voyage Round My Father"), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2019)[1]
Erich Fried, Austrian-British writer, born in Vienna (d. 1988)
Freddy Randall, English jazz trumpeter, born in London (d. 1999)
Robert Fell, British civil servant and the first paid chief executive of the London Stock Exchange (1975-81), born in England (d. 2015)
Alan Ross, British poet and editor (London Magazine), born in Calcutta, India (d. 2001)
Camille Laurin, Quebec psychiatrist and politician (Parti Québécois), born in Charlemagne, Quebec (d. 1999)
Carlos Moorhead, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from California), born in Long Beach, California (d. 2011)
John Ernest, American constructionist artist, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1994)
Pat Harder, American NFL fullback (Cardinals, Lions), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1992)
Vladimir Etush, Soviet actor and People's Artist of the USSR, born in Moscow, RSFSR (d. 2019)
Chih Ree Sun, Chinese-American physicist and poet, born in Anhui, Republic of China (d. 2007)[1]
Guiseppe Martelli, Italian physicist, born in Pistoia, Italy (d. 1994)
Denny Wright, British jazz guitarist, born in Bromley, Kent, England (d. 1992)
Mimi Benzell, American operatic soprano (Gilda-Rigoletto), born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 1970)
Patricia Kennedy Lawford, American socialite and sister of President John F. Kennedy, born in Brookline, Massachusetts (d. 2006)
Patrick Meaney, British businessman and CEO (Rank Organization), born in Wandsworth, London (d. 1992)
Gilles Grégoire, Quebec politician (co-founder of the Parti Québécois), born in Quebec City, Quebec (d. 2006)
John Hamilton-Jones, British CEO (Richmond Enterprises) and British Major-General, born in England (d. 1997)
Johnny Bragg, American doo-wop singer-songwriter (The Prisonaires - "Just Walkin' in the Rain"), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 2004)
Marguerite Piazza, American operatic soprano (Young Broadway), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2012)
Ettore Manni, Italian actor (Street People, Divine Nymph, Fatal Desire, Heroes in Hell), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1979)
Michael Frederick, Barbadian cricketer (one Test WI v England 1954, scored 0 & 30), born in St Peter, Barbados (d. 2014)
